items.q: Does a higher magnitude number mean a brighter star?
items.correct: No - smaller and even negative numbers are brighter on the magnitude scale.
items.misconception: Yes, a bigger number means brighter.
items.why: The scale runs backwards: magnitude 1 is brighter than magnitude 6, and negatives are brightest.

items.q: Why do stars appear to move across the sky at night?
items.correct: Because the Earth rotates on its axis.
items.misconception: Because the stars physically orbit the Earth each night.
items.random_wrong: Because the sky is a solid moving dome.
items.why: Diurnal motion is an illusion of Earth's spin, not real motion of the distant stars.

items.correct: No - it is an asterism within the constellation Ursa Major.
items.misconception: Yes, it is its own constellation.
items.random_wrong: It is a small nearby galaxy.
items.why: Asterisms are recognisable patterns that are not official constellations; the Plough sits inside Ursa Major.

items.q: On the magnitude scale, a brighter star has a:
items.why: Correct answer: Smaller (or even negative) number. “Larger number” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: The Plough is best described as:
items.why: Correct answer: An asterism. “A constellation” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: The nightly east-to-west motion of the stars is caused by:
items.why: Correct answer: The Earth's rotation. “The stars orbiting Earth” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A circumpolar star is one that:
items.why: Correct answer: Never sets for that observer. “Never rises” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What is the approximate faintest apparent magnitude visible to the unaided eye in a dark sky?
items.why: Exact answer: 6 magnitude. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
